# Questions in Livestream 193

Live Stream #193: Back in the Saddle.

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=624" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">10:24</a> | What should we make of the reported altar discovery near Jerusalem that has been linked to David and the First Temple period? | He said it may be a real First Temple period ritual site, but not a Davidic one, since the evidence sounded more 8th century BC than David's time. | He identified the object as probably a libation altar rather than a burnt-offering or incense altar, accepted that it could be a legitimate First Temple period cultic installation, and rejected the David link because the reported date range points well after David. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=750" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">12:30</a> | How long does it take for a destroyed city to become buried underground? | He said Near Eastern tells can begin forming within a few centuries, though cities are usually being buried into a mound rather than simply sinking beneath the ground. | He explained that abandoned or rebuilt cities accumulate debris, mudbrick collapse, and later occupation layers, so the process is more like a tell gradually rising over centuries than a city simply disappearing below flat ground. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=879" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">14:39</a> | Is there direct or indirect evidence that Joseph could have been vizier in the Hyksos period? | He said yes, arguing that details such as Joseph's use of a chariot fit a Hyksos-period setting better than many later models. | He treated the evidence as circumstantial rather than a named inscription for Joseph, emphasizing that the Genesis details fit a Second Intermediate Period context where chariots and Semitic influence in Egypt make better sense. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=1159" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">19:19</a> | Is it significant that the parallel to 1 Kings 6:1 in 2 Chronicles 3:2 does not include the 480 years? | He said not really, warning against building an argument from absence and noting the two texts are doing different things. | He argued that Chronicles and Kings are not obligated to repeat every chronological datum, so the omission of the 480 years is not strong evidence against the number or against the Kings chronology. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=1439" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">23:59</a> | What will be the greatest theological or philosophical issue for the next generation of Christians? | He said soteriology will be central, especially how changing moral assumptions relate to sin, grace, and God's plan of salvation. | He framed the coming challenge around salvation because shifting ethical assumptions affect how people define sin, why salvation is needed, and whether Christian doctrine can still speak coherently about judgment, grace, and redemption. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=1805" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">30:05</a> | How can the church overcome emotionalism? | He said expository preaching is the main antidote, because churches built only on emotional stimulation produce shallow roots. | He contrasted emotionally driven church experiences with steady teaching through Scripture, saying people need deep biblical roots so faith is not dependent on music, atmosphere, mysticism, or repeated emotional highs. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=2359" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">39:19</a> | What is case law, and how can someone be shown that Old Testament law often works that way? | He said Old Testament legal material frequently gives case examples with maximum penalties rather than exhaustive statutory code. | He pointed to ancient Near Eastern law collections as case-law parallels and said biblical laws often present sample cases with maximum penalties while allowing judges to apply lesser punishments or adapt principles to similar situations. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=2647" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">44:07</a> | Who is the second "he" in Exodus 34:28? | He said the antecedent chain points to Moses writing the second set of tablets, while the first set was written by the finger of God. | He distinguished the two tablet episodes: Exodus 31 describes the first tablets as written by God, while in Exodus 34 the grammar and narrative flow indicate Moses writing the replacement tablets under God's instruction. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=2799" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">46:39</a> | Why do the NIV and NLT translate Milcom as Molech in 1 Kings 11:5? | He traced it to a traditional rendering, likely influenced by older confusion between Milcom and Molech rather than the Hebrew text itself. | He said the Hebrew name in the verse is Milcom, the Ammonite deity, and that some translations likely preserve an older interpretive tradition that blurred Milcom with Molech rather than reflecting a necessary textual reading. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=3180" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">53:00</a> | Is Astarte the same as Asherah? | No. He distinguished Astarte from Asherah and said the relationship of Asherah traditions across the Levant is more complicated. | He treated Astarte and Asherah as distinct goddesses, while noting that Asherah material varies across regions and periods, so the answer cannot be reduced to one simple equivalence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=3298" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">54:58</a> | In Exodus 40:18, why did Moses erect the tabernacle? | He said it was a first-time exception before the tabernacle and priests were consecrated; afterward, that work belonged to the designated Levitical servants. | He explained that Moses sets up the tabernacle at its initial consecration because the priestly and Levitical system has not yet begun operating in its normal pattern; later transport and setup duties are assigned differently. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=3467" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">57:47</a> | Was Moses considered a priest? | No. He said Moses was considered a prophet, while Aaron and Aaron's line were designated for priestly service. | He rejected calling Moses a priest in the formal Israelite sense, distinguishing Moses' unique prophetic and mediating role from the hereditary priesthood given to Aaron's family. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=3524" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">58:44</a> | How should we interpret Jeremiah being longer in the Masoretic Text than in the Septuagint, and what about the Qumran manuscripts? | He said he would need to examine the evidence more closely and noted that Qumran may help, but he had not studied that issue enough to decide. | He avoided giving a firm answer, saying the Jeremiah textual tradition is a specialized issue and that Qumran evidence could matter, but he had not worked through the data enough to offer a responsible judgment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=3713" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:01:53</a> | What does "soothing aroma" mean regarding sacrifices? | He said it refers to God being pleased to have relationship with his people through properly offered sacrifices that cover sin in the old system. | He explained the phrase as covenantal and relational language: when Israel offers sacrifices rightly, God is pleased with the restored relationship and with the system of atonement rather than literally needing the smell of food. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=3952" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:05:52</a> | Does Moses parallel himself as Horus and Pharaoh as Set at the beginning of Exodus? | He answered no, saying the Exodus narrative is doing something else with the Moses-Pharaoh contrast. | He rejected the Horus-Set parallel and said the narrative contrast is better read within Exodus' own theological framing, not as Moses casting himself into an Egyptian mythic rivalry. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=4310" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:11:50</a> | Could Zipporah have been Midianite by nationality and Kushite by ethnic heritage? | He said no, arguing that Kushites and Midianites should not be conflated and that attempts to connect Zipporah to the Cushite wife in Numbers 12 are weak. | He said the proposed ethnic combination depends on forcing separate groups together, and he treated the Numbers 12 Cushite wife issue as a poor basis for identifying Zipporah as ethnically Kushite. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=4506" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:15:06</a> | What was the job of the Medjay; were they like Egyptian sheriffs? | He described the Medjay as peace officers, guards, constables, and community leaders trusted by Pharaoh rather than as assassins or modern sheriffs. | He said the Medjay had policing, guarding, and local leadership functions, sometimes serving as trusted agents of the state, but he cautioned against flattening them into modern law-enforcement or pop-culture categories. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=4992" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:23:12</a> | Was ancient racism more about descent than appearance? | He said that is probably right: Egyptian xenophobia was real, but it was more tied to origin, identity, and group descent than modern racial categories. | He distinguished ancient hostility toward outsiders from modern race categories, saying Egyptian attitudes focused more on people-group identity, origin, and descent than on skin color alone. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=5232" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:27:12</a> | How long would Moses have been weaned by his mother? | He said the exact answer is unknown, but Egyptian weaning could plausibly have taken several years, perhaps around three to five. | He said the text does not give Moses' exact age, but ancient and Egyptian child-rearing patterns make a multi-year nursing period plausible before the child was handed over to Pharaoh's daughter. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=5522" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:32:02</a> | In Leviticus 2:11-12, what is wrong with leaven and honey? | He said leaven was associated with mold and decay, while raw honey could contain insect parts and was not ritually appropriate for offerings. | He connected leaven with fermentation, mold, and decay and treated honey as ritually unsuitable for altar smoke because raw honey could contain contaminants, even if both substances could appear in other contexts. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=5900" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:38:20</a> | What is the salt of the covenant in Leviticus 2:13? | He said the literal salt in offerings symbolizes the covenant's preserving and purifying function, not that the covenant itself is literal salt. | He explained that salt is actually included with the offering, but its covenant significance is symbolic: it evokes preservation, purification, and durability within the sacrificial relationship. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=6355" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:45:55</a> | Were sin and guilt offerings common in the ancient Near East, and what was their purpose? | He said offerings were common, but Israel's sin and guilt offerings differed from feeding-the-gods systems because they dealt with covering sin, guilt, purification, vows, and thanksgiving. | He said sacrificial systems were widespread, but Israel's offerings were not about sustaining deities with food; they served covenantal purposes such as atonement, purification, restitution, thanksgiving, and renewed relationship with God. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=6539" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:48:59</a> | What does it mean to say the Levites are the true Israelites? | He said the phrase depends on who is using it, can become loaded language, and is not a good factual way to describe Israelite identity. | He treated the phrase as rhetorically charged rather than historically precise, because Levites are one Israelite group with a specific cultic role, not the only authentic Israelites in a simple ethnic sense. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=6643" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:50:43</a> | What crisis occurred in Egypt during Joseph's lifetime? | He pointed to the famine in Genesis, with Joseph managing storage and distribution through that crisis. | He tied the question directly to the Genesis famine narrative, where Joseph's administrative role is to prepare storehouses during abundance and distribute grain during the regional food crisis. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=6836" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:53:56</a> | Who is Ishtar, and when was she worshiped? | He identified Ishtar as the Assyrian name for Inanna and connected Astarte with the Levantine form of that broader goddess tradition. | He described Ishtar or Inanna as a Mesopotamian goddess associated with love and war, connected Astarte as a related Levantine form, and said the cult declines especially with the spread of Christianity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=7058" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:57:38</a> | How does ethnic geography relate to Anatolia, "east," and the Hittites? | He said Greek and Israelite ethnic geographies use different reference points, so Anatolia as "east" reflects Greek geography rather than Israelite usage. | He explained that Anatolia is east from a Greek perspective because it lies east of Greece, but biblical and Israelite geographic categories use a different map of peoples and directions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=7224" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:00:24</a> | Were the Hittites around during the time of Jesus? | He said not as a political kingdom, though remnants of Hittite religion may have survived into the early Christian centuries. | He said the Hittite empire and political identity were long gone by Jesus' day, but cultural or religious survivals from old Hittite traditions may have persisted in some regions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=7372" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:02:52</a> | Who was your best beta reader, and why was it Mama? | He said each beta reader brought something different, with Mama especially good at detecting subtext and others helping with plot threads and other issues. | He answered playfully but then explained that different beta readers catch different things: Mama reads subtext well, another reader tracks plot, and multiple readers reduce blind spots in fiction editing. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=8398" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:19:58</a> | Why do people trash Leviticus when it is rewarding to read? | He said many readers react against Leviticus because they misunderstand its genre and legal setting rather than reading it on its own terms. | He said Leviticus is often dismissed because modern readers approach it with the wrong expectations, missing how ritual, holiness, sacrifice, and case-law patterns function inside Israel's covenant life. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=8537" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:22:17</a> | How did Egyptians view the religions of other nations? | He said Egyptians often incorporated foreign deities into their own pantheon, including gods from Nubia and the Levant. | He described Egyptian religion as capable of absorbing foreign gods, especially from neighboring regions, so Egyptians could recognize or incorporate outside deities without abandoning their own pantheon. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=8747" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:25:47</a> | Why would the Romans want Jesus' bloody garment in Matthew 27:35? | He said clothing was very valuable, Romans would not be ritually concerned about the blood, and the garments could be washed and reused or sold. | He emphasized the economic value of clothing in antiquity: executioners could claim garments as usable property, and Roman soldiers would not share Jewish purity concerns about blood. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=9183" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:33:03</a> | Is Progressive Christianity really Christianity? | He answered no, arguing that it denies core doctrines and functions more like Christian-flavored moralism than historic Christianity. | He argued that Progressive Christianity commonly rejects doctrines he sees as central, including sin and salvation, and therefore becomes a moral or cultural movement using Christian language rather than orthodox Christianity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=9306" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:35:06</a> | Did ancient Egyptians celebrate birthdays? | He said ordinary Egyptians do not seem to have celebrated birthdays in the way modern people do, though rulers could mark such occasions differently. | He said Egyptians knew ages but were not generally focused on birthdays or anniversaries as modern celebrations, while later or royal contexts may show different kinds of commemoration. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=9421" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:37:01</a> | Can you go over chronological methods for dating Pharaohs' reigns versus the Hittite sack of Babylon? | He discussed synchronisms, king lists, regnal years, treaties, wine dockets, and the uncertainty ranges that differ across Egyptian, Babylonian, and Hittite chronologies. | He described chronology as a network of evidence rather than one calculation, combining Egyptian regnal data and administrative records with Near Eastern synchronisms while acknowledging that different systems carry different uncertainty ranges. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=10072" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:47:52</a> | Has your paper on the grain-price knowledge been published yet? | Not yet. He said he had heard from the editor and had to upload the correct paper. | He said the paper was not yet published, but the editorial process had moved forward and the immediate issue was getting the correct file uploaded. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=10606" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:56:46</a> | Does your chronology account for plus-or-minus dating ranges from sites and radiocarbon work? | Yes. He said the model incorporates asymmetrical error bars rather than pretending chronology can be calculated with false precision. | He said his chronological model does include margins of error, including uneven or asymmetrical ranges, because archaeological and radiocarbon dates do not provide exact single-year precision. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=11017" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:03:37</a> | Is there Christological development in the Gospels? | He said yes, but from a high-Christology view it means an unveiling of who Christ is, not Christ becoming divine over time. | He allowed development in presentation and recognition across the Gospels, but rejected a low-to-high evolutionary model in which Jesus gradually becomes divine in Christian belief. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=11125" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:05:25</a> | Have you heard of the Dead South, and if so, what are your thoughts? | He had not heard of the group and could not offer an opinion. | He said he was unfamiliar with the group, so he did not evaluate their music or offer a substantive judgment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=11580" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:13:00</a> | Can you steelman Isaiah 53 not being about Christ? | He said he could not do that credibly because he finds the passage too strongly Messianic, though he acknowledged that some rabbis and others argue otherwise. | He said he could describe opposing Jewish or non-Christian readings, but he personally finds Isaiah 53 so strongly connected to the Messiah and Christ that a true steelman would be hard for him to present honestly. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=11854" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:17:34</a> | Could Elizabeth have taken a Nazirite vow for John the Baptist? | He said she would not be taking the vow in the ordinary personal sense, but John could still have been dedicated as a Nazirite. | He distinguished a mother's vow from the individual's own Nazirite status, saying Elizabeth would not simply take the vow on John's behalf, though John could be set apart from birth in a Nazirite-like way. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=12078" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:21:18</a> | Is God's abode above the waters or part of the expanse in Genesis 1? | He said the question presses the cosmological language too literally; Genesis depicts God's spirit over the waters during creation but does not locate God's spiritual abode inside the created expanse. | He said Genesis uses creation imagery about waters and the expanse, but God's actual abode is spiritual and not a physical layer inside the created heavens. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=12480" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:28:00</a> | Is it significant that the creation account does not explicitly mention the spiritual realm and spiritual beings the way other religions do? | He said Genesis uses analogical language and does address the spiritual realm, but not in the explicit mythic style some other texts use. | He argued that Genesis deliberately frames creation differently from surrounding myths, speaking about God and spiritual realities without giving the same kind of detailed mythological cast of divine beings. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=13549" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:45:49</a> | How accurate is it to use lunisolar chronology and calendars? | He said the accuracy depends on the calendar's intercalation system; if it keeps the calendar aligned with the solar year, it can be reasonable. | He said a lunisolar calendar can work well if it uses intercalation to keep lunar months from drifting away from the solar seasons; without that correction, accuracy declines over time. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=13660" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:47:40</a> | What is the theology behind criminals being buried in unconsecrated ground and Christians in blessed ground near a church? | He said that depends entirely on the denomination, because burial rules and theology vary by tradition. | He did not give one universal doctrine, because different Christian traditions attach different meanings and rules to consecrated burial, churchyard burial, and exclusion from those spaces. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=13845" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:50:45</a> | What Bible details surprise you when rereading? | He pointed to details such as Judah being the largest contingent in both censuses in Numbers, while noting such details become striking on rereading. | He said repeated reading makes overlooked details stand out, giving Judah's consistently large numbers in the Numbers censuses as the kind of pattern that becomes noticeable with familiarity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=14393" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:59:53</a> | Could "Ur of the Chaldees" be a redaction by scribes? | He said it could be an error or emendation, but he would be hesitant to call it a redaction because that implies an intentional content-changing alteration. | He distinguished possible textual updating or error from redaction, saying redaction language suggests a deliberate theological or narrative alteration and should not be used too loosely. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=14570" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:02:50</a> | What goal would a scribe have in changing Avaris to Rameses? | He said early Exodus date advocates might argue it was an updating of an unfamiliar place name, but he thinks that kind of redaction theory creates larger textual problems. | He said the usual argument would be that a later scribe modernized an old place name for readers, but he thinks that move creates problems for textual reliability and for other Exodus route names. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=14675" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:04:35</a> | For the early Exodus redaction theory, what would Avaris look like in typical Hebrew or Amorite? | He said Avaris and Rameses do not connect well phonetically or etymologically, and other route names create similar problems for the theory. | He argued that Avaris does not naturally turn into Rameses in Hebrew or Amorite terms, so the redaction theory must explain more than a simple name update. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=15097" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:11:37</a> | If a self-identified Christian scholar refuses to affirm publicly that Christ rose from the dead, should they be considered Christian? | He answered no, because belief in the resurrection of Christ is central to Christian identity. | He said public refusal to affirm the resurrection is incompatible with Christian identity because the resurrection is not an optional doctrine but central to the gospel. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=15187" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:13:07</a> | How should Deuteronomy 22:28-29 be distinguished from Deuteronomy 22:25-26? | He said 22:25-26 describes a non-consensual field assault with a presumption of the woman's innocence, while 22:28-29 concerns a discovered consensual encounter. | He explained the distinction through the legal scenarios: the field case assumes the woman cried out but could not be rescued, while the later case concerns an encounter that is discovered and carries different social and legal consequences. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=15960" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:26:00</a> | In Numbers 5:17, what is the holy water? | He explained it as water used in the priestly ordeal, combined with dust from the tabernacle floor in the ritual context. | He said the holy water is set-apart water used in the jealousy ordeal, probably maintained for priestly ritual use, and Numbers 5 combines it with tabernacle-floor dust as part of the ceremony. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=16122" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:28:42</a> | What are your thoughts on Jordan Peterson's <em>We Who Wrestle with God</em>? | He had not read it, so he declined to comment on the book's argument. | He explicitly withheld judgment because he had not read the book, so the transcript supports only a no-comment answer rather than an evaluation of Peterson's argument. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=16153" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:29:13</a> | What are your thoughts on Wesley Huff's debate with Billy Carson? | He thought the debate went badly for Carson, while noting that public apologetics also brings scrutiny and pressure on Huff. | He said Huff performed strongly and Carson's arguments fared poorly, but he also cautioned that sudden public attention can bring intense criticism and pressure on an apologist. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=16346" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:32:26</a> | What are the ten toes in Daniel referring to? | He treated the passage as an eschatological prophecy question and handled it cautiously because such passages are interpretively difficult. | He approached Daniel's image cautiously, identifying the toes as part of a difficult end-times interpretive framework rather than giving a confident one-to-one identification. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=17153" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:45:53</a> | How do you foresee AI affecting academic scholarship, archaeology, and biblical exegesis? | He called AI a tool, expected more plagiarism problems in scholarship, and thought archaeology would be less directly affected than language-processing work. | He said AI will be useful but dangerous in academic writing because it can accelerate plagiarism or shallow work; archaeology depends more on fieldwork, while exegesis and language-heavy scholarship are more exposed. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=17276" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:47:56</a> | What should be done when a scholar repeatedly gets the scholarship wrong on an issue? | He said the main thing is to stick to the evidence; persistent refusal to correct errors may warrant public critique. | He advised focusing on evidence rather than personalities, but said repeated public errors and refusal to correct them can justify direct public response. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=17442" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:50:42</a> | What are your thoughts on the Elephantine papyri? | He said they are an important source for Israelite culture in Egypt, especially for understanding an exiled Israelite community. | He valued the Elephantine papyri because they show a Jewish or Israelite community living in Egypt and provide concrete evidence for their religious and social practices outside the land. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=17542" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:52:22</a> | How did Hellenistic Jews view pagan gods? | He said post-exilic Jews had learned from the exile and generally were not inclined to return to polytheism, even while engaging the surrounding world. | He said Jews in the Hellenistic period could interact with pagan cultures, but the post-exilic lesson against idolatry meant they generally did not view pagan gods as acceptable objects of worship. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=17816" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:56:56</a> | Are Philistines and Phoenicians of the same stock, and how should they be distinguished? | He distinguished Philistines as Sea Peoples and Phoenicians as a Canaanite-related coastal population with different cultural development. | He said Philistines are best understood among the Sea Peoples on the southern coastal plain, while Phoenicians develop from Canaanite coastal populations farther north with a different cultural profile. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/F3BEjnAqgWw?t=18097" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">5:01:37</a> | How did David overcome stage fright or fear of public speaking as a teenager? | He said he joined Toastmasters and recommended that kind of practice for becoming comfortable speaking publicly. | He credited repeated structured practice, specifically Toastmasters, as the way he became more comfortable with public speaking and recommended practical exposure rather than a purely theoretical fix. |
